critic_raw:
The experiment uses 5 years of EHR data with a stratified random split, justified by 'short-term temporal autocorrelations in patient readmission outcomes are sufficiently weak.' This is the weakest justification in the dataset: 5-year spans include major shifts in hospital practices, coding standards (ICD-9 to ICD-10 transition, if applicable), patient demographics, and treatment guidelines. Even if individual patient autocorrelation is weak, the population distribution shifts over 5 years, and a model trained on 2019 patient data evaluated on 2019 patient data (through random mixing) produces an overly optimistic estimate of performance on 2024 patients.

The RNN is 'selected for its potential to capture temporal dependencies.' The design does not specify what the temporal sequence being modeled is — is it the sequence of diagnoses within a hospitalization, the sequence of hospitalizations over time, or the sequence of vitals during the admission? These are fundamentally different modeling problems requiring different architectures. Without specifying the input sequence structure, the experiment cannot be reproduced and the RNN's inductive bias cannot be evaluated.

The design mentions 'multi-layer RNN' without specifying depth, hidden units, whether LSTM or GRU cells are used, or dropout configuration. These architectural decisions substantially affect both expressivity and generalization, and without them, 'equivalent hyperparameter tuning' to logistic regression is asymmetrically constrained — logistic regression has few hyperparameters (regularization type and strength), while the RNN has many (depth, width, cell type, dropout, learning rate, sequence length). 'Equivalent effort' is not equivalent search space.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'grid search' for tuning but the RNN tuning method is not specified. Grid search and random/Bayesian search explore hyperparameter spaces differently. If the RNN uses a more effective search method, the comparison favors the RNN through search quality rather than model quality.

Socio-economic status and outpatient care access are included as confound controls via 'comprehensive feature inclusion,' but these features are typically incomplete in EHR data — SES is often inferred from zip code, and outpatient follow-up is frequently undocumented. Including noisy proxies for these confounds in the feature set and claiming confound control is circular — the model may learn the noise in these proxies as features rather than the underlying confound structure.
all_issues_raised: 5-year temporal range makes 'weak temporal autocorrelation' implausible at the population level — hospital practices, coding standards, and treatment guidelines shift over this period, and random mixing evaluates within-era generalization rather than forward-looking deployment performance.

The temporal sequence structure being modeled by the RNN is not specified (intra-admission diagnosis sequences vs. inter-admission history vs. continuous vitals), making the architecture choice unverifiable and the experiment unreproducible.

The RNN has a much larger hyperparameter space than logistic regression; 'equivalent tuning effort' operationalized as grid search is asymmetrically constrained and likely under-explores the RNN's space, either understating RNN performance or making fair comparison impossible.

Socio-economic confound controls rely on incomplete EHR proxies (zip code for SES, undocumented outpatient follow-up) — including noisy proxies as features and claiming confound control is circular, as the model may fit proxy noise rather than the underlying confound.

The RNN's tuning method is unspecified while the baseline uses explicit grid search — different search strategies for the two models introduce a methodological asymmetry that cannot be attributed to model architecture differences.
all_issues_adjudicated: 5-year temporal range makes 'weak temporal autocorrelation' implausible at the population level — hospital practices, coding standards, and treatment guidelines shift over this period, and random mixing evaluates within-era generalization rather than forward-looking deployment performance.

The temporal sequence structure being modeled by the RNN is not specified (intra-admission diagnosis sequences vs. inter-admission history vs. continuous vitals), making the architecture choice unverifiable and the experiment unreproducible.

The RNN has a much larger hyperparameter space than logistic regression; 'equivalent tuning effort' operationalized as grid search is asymmetrically constrained and likely under-explores the RNN's space, either understating RNN performance or making fair comparison impossible.

Socio-economic confound controls rely on incomplete EHR proxies (zip code for SES, undocumented outpatient follow-up) — including noisy proxies as features and claiming confound control is circular, as the model may fit proxy noise rather than the underlying confound.

The RNN's tuning method is unspecified while the baseline uses explicit grid search — different search strategies for the two models introduce a methodological asymmetry that cannot be attributed to model architecture differences.
